title: Certain Presbyters burnt in a Ship by Order of Valens. Famine in Phrygia.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Certain pious men of the clerical order, eighty in number, among whom Urbanus, Theodore, and Menedemus were the leaders, proceeded to Nicomedia, and there presented to the emperor a supplicatory petition, informing him and complaining of the ill-usage to which they had been subjected.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The emperor was filled with wrath; but dissembled his displeasure in their presence, and gave Modestus the prefect a secret order to apprehend these persons, and put them to death.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The manner in which they were destroyed being unusual, deserves to be recorded.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The prefect fearing that he should excite the populace to a seditious movement against himself, if he attempted the public execution of so many, pretended to send the men away into exile.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Accordingly as they received the intelligence of their destiny with great firmness of mind the prefect ordered that they should be embarked as if to be conveyed to their several places of banishment, having meanwhile enjoined on the sailors to set the vessel on fire, as soon as they reached the mid sea, that their victims being so destroyed, might even be deprived of burial.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This injunction was obeyed; for when they arrived at the middle of the Astacian Gulf, the crew set fire to the ship, and then took refuge in a small barque which followed them, and so escaped.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Meanwhile it came to pass that a strong easterly wind blew, and the burning ship was roughly driven but moved faster and was preserved until it reached a port named Dacidizus, where it was utterly consumed together with the men who were shut up in it.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Many have asserted that this impious deed was not suffered to go unpunished: for there immediately after arose so great a famine throughout all Phrygia, that a large proportion of the inhabitants were obliged to abandon their country for a time, and betake themselves some to Constantinople and some to other provinces.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For Constantinople, notwithstanding the vast population it supplies, yet always abounds with the necessaries of life, all manner of provisions being imported into it by sea from various regions; and the Euxine which lies near it, furnishes it with wheat to any extent it may require.
